message.content: Hi. This is the tidbit-backend. Take a look at the code

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	package main
2	
3	import (
4		"net/http"
5		"os"
6		"strconv"
7	
8		"tidbit-backend/internal/weather"
9	
10		"github.com/gin-gonic/gin"
11	)
12	
13	func apiKeyMiddleware() gin.HandlerFunc {
14	    return func(c *gin.Context) {
15	        key := c.GetHeader("X-API-Key")
16	        if key != os.Getenv("API_KEY") {
17	            c.JSON(http.StatusUnauthorized, gin.H{"error": "unauthorized"})
18	            c.Abort()
19	            return
20	        }
21	        c.Next()
22	    }
23	}
24	
25	func main() {
26	    weatherClient := weather.NewWeatherClient()
27	
28	    r := gin.Default()
29	
30	    r.GET("/api/v1/weather", apiKeyMiddleware(), func(c *gin.Context) {
31	        latStr := c.Query("lat")
32	        lonStr := c.Query("lon")
33	
34	        lat, err := strconv.ParseFloat(latStr, 64)
35	        if err != nil {
36	            c.JSON(http.StatusBadRequest, gin.H{"error": "invalid latitude"})
37	            return
38	        }
39	
40	        lon, err := strconv.ParseFloat(lonStr, 64)
41	        if err != nil {
42	            c.JSON(http.StatusBadRequest, gin.H{"error": "invalid longitude"})
43	            return
44	        }
45	
46	        result, err := weatherClient.GetWeather(weather.WeatherRequest{
47	            Latitude:  lat,
48	            Longitude: lon,
49	        })
50	        if err != nil {
51	            c.JSON(http.StatusInternalServerError, gin.H{"error": err.Error()})
52	            return
53	        }
54	
55	        c.JSON(http.StatusOK, result)
56	    })
57	
58	    
59	    r.Run(":6769")
60	}

1	package weather
2	
3	import (
4	    "encoding/json"
5	    "fmt"
6	    "net/http"
7	    "strconv"
8	    "time"
9	)
10	
11	const (
12	    requestTimeout = 10 * time.Second
13	    maxAttempts    = 3
14	    retryDelay     = 500 * time.Millisecond
15	)
16	
17	type WeatherClient struct {
18	    BaseURL    string
19	    HTTPClient *http.Client
20	}
21	
22	func NewWeatherClient() *WeatherClient {
23	    return &WeatherClient{
24	        BaseURL:    "https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast",
25	        HTTPClient: &http.Client{Timeout: requestTimeout},
26	    }
27	}
28	
29	func (w *WeatherClient) GetWeather(req WeatherRequest) (*WeatherResponse, error) {
30	    url := fmt.Sprintf(
31	        "%s?latitude=%s&longitude=%s&current=temperature_2m,apparent_temperature,is_day,weather_code,relative_humidity_2m&daily=temperature_2m_max,temperature_2m_min&hourly=uv_index,precipitation_probability&timezone=auto&forecast_days=1&wind_speed_unit=mph&temperature_unit=fahrenheit&precipitation_unit=inch",
32	        w.BaseURL,
33	        strconv.FormatFloat(req.Latitude, 'f', 4, 64),
34	        strconv.FormatFloat(req.Longitude, 'f', 4, 64),
35	    )
36	
37	    var lastErr error
38	    for attempt := 1; attempt <= maxAttempts; attempt++ {
39	        raw, err := w.fetch(url)
40	        if err == nil {
41	            return parseResponse(raw), nil
42	        }
43	        lastErr = err
44	        if attempt < maxAttempts {
45	            time.Sleep(retryDelay * time.Duration(attempt))
46	        }
47	    }
48	
49	    return nil, lastErr
50	}
51	
52	func (w *WeatherClient) fetch(url string) (openMeteoResponse, error) {
53	    var raw openMeteoResponse
54	
55	    resp, err := w.HTTPClient.Get(url)
56	    if err != nil {
57	        return raw, fmt.Errorf("failed to call open-meteo: %w", err)
58	    }
59	    defer resp.Body.Close()
60	
61	    if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
62	        return raw, fmt.Errorf("unexpected status from open-meteo: %d", resp.StatusCode)
63	    }
64	
65	    if err := json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&raw); err != nil {
66	        return raw, fmt.Errorf("failed to decode response: %w", err)
67	    }
68	
69	    return raw, nil
70	}
71	
72	func parseResponse(raw openMeteoResponse) *WeatherResponse {
73	    currentHour, _ := strconv.Atoi(raw.Current.Time[11:13])
74	
75	    return &WeatherResponse{
76	        CurrentTemperature: int(raw.Current.Temperature),
77	        FeelsLike:          int(raw.Current.ApparentTemperature),
78	        IsDay:              raw.Current.IsDay,
79	        WeatherCode:        raw.Current.WeatherCode,
80	        Humidity:           int(raw.Current.Humidity),
81	        MinTemp:            int(raw.Daily.MinTemp[0]),
82	        MaxTemp:            int(raw.Daily.MaxTemp[0]),
83	        PrecipProbability:  int(raw.Hourly.PrecipProbability[currentHour]),
84	        UVIndex:            int(raw.Hourly.UVIndex[currentHour]),
85	        TZOffset:           raw.UtcOffsetSeconds / 3600,
86	    }
87	}

1	package weather
2	
3	type WeatherRequest struct {
4	    Latitude  float64
5	    Longitude float64
6	}
7	
8	type openMeteoResponse struct {
9	    UtcOffsetSeconds int                `json:"utc_offset_seconds"`
10	    Current          openMeteoCurrent   `json:"current"`
11	    Daily            openMeteoDaily     `json:"daily"`
12	    Hourly           openMeteoHourly    `json:"hourly"`
13	}
14	
15	type openMeteoCurrent struct {
16	    Time                string  `json:"time"`
17	    Temperature         float64 `json:"temperature_2m"`
18	    ApparentTemperature float64 `json:"apparent_temperature"`
19	    IsDay               int     `json:"is_day"`
20	    WeatherCode         int     `json:"weather_code"`
21	    Humidity            float64 `json:"relative_humidity_2m"`
22	}
23	
24	type openMeteoDaily struct {
25	    MaxTemp []float64 `json:"temperature_2m_max"`
26	    MinTemp []float64 `json:"temperature_2m_min"`
27	}
28	
29	type openMeteoHourly struct {
30	    UVIndex              []float64 `json:"uv_index"`
31	    PrecipProbability    []float64 `json:"precipitation_probability"`
32	}
33	
34	type WeatherResponse struct {
35	    CurrentTemperature  int `json:"current_temperature"`
36	    FeelsLike           int `json:"feels_like_temperature"`
37	    IsDay               int `json:"is_day"`
38	    WeatherCode         int `json:"weather_code"`
39	    Humidity            int `json:"humidity"`
40	    MinTemp             int `json:"min_temp"`
41	    MaxTemp             int `json:"max_temp"`
42	    PrecipProbability   int `json:"precip_probability"`
43	    UVIndex             int `json:"uv_index"`
44	    TZOffset            int `json:"tz_offset"`
45	}

content.text:
I've read through it — it's a compact codebase: `cmd/server/main.go` (Gin setup + auth), `internal/weather/handler.go` (Open-Meteo client with retries), and `internal/weather/models.go` (upstream decode structs + the flattened response). The code matches what CLAUDE.md describes. A few observations while I was in there:

**Auth footgun**: `apiKeyMiddleware` compares the header to `os.Getenv("API_KEY")` directly (`main.go:16`). If `API_KEY` is ever unset or empty, a request with *no* `X-API-Key` header passes, since `"" == ""`. A guard that rejects when the env var is empty would close that. (The comparison is also not constant-time, though for a hobby LED display that's academic.)

**Panic risk in `parseResponse`** (`handler.go:72-87`): it indexes `raw.Current.Time[11:13]`, `Daily.MinTemp[0]`, and `Hourly.UVIndex[currentHour]` without bounds checks. If Open-Meteo ever returns a 200 with an unexpected/empty body, the handler panics — Gin's recovery middleware turns that into a 500 rather than crashing the process, but you'd get no useful error message.

**`PORT` env var is ignored**: the server hardcodes `r.Run(":6769")` (`main.go:59`), but the Docker docs in CLAUDE.md pass `-e PORT=6769` as if it were configurable. Harmless as-is, just misleading.

**Retry logic retries everything**: the recent "more resilient" retry loop in `GetWeather` retries on any error, including 4xx responses (e.g. invalid coordinates), where retrying can't succeed. Minor waste, not a bug.

Otherwise it's in good shape — clean separation between the upstream decode shape and the public response, sensible timeout on the HTTP client, and the hour-slicing trick for indexing hourly arrays works because `timezone=auto` makes the ISO timestamps local. There are no tests yet, as CLAUDE.md notes.

Want me to fix any of these (the empty-API_KEY guard and the bounds checks would be the two I'd prioritize), or were you looking at something else?

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'

content.text: I'll fix the empty-key footgun: fail fast at startup if `API_KEY` isn't set, and use a constant-time comparison in the middleware.

content.content: The file /Users/jackpotter/projects/tidbit-backend/cmd/server/main.go has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +        log.Fatal("API_KEY environment variable must be set")

content.content: The file /Users/jackpotter/projects/tidbit-backend/cmd/server/main.go has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
The API key check is updated and the build passes. Two changes in `cmd/server/main.go`:

- `main` now reads `API_KEY` once at startup and exits with `log.Fatal` if it's empty — previously an unset env var meant any request with no `X-API-Key` header would authenticate (`"" == ""`).
- The middleware takes the key as a parameter and compares with `subtle.ConstantTimeCompare` instead of `!=`.

**On your question — how `subtle` works:** `crypto/subtle` is a small stdlib package of operations whose *execution time doesn't depend on the values being compared*. `ConstantTimeCompare(x, y)` returns 1 if the two byte slices are equal, 0 otherwise, but instead of bailing out at the first mismatched byte the way `==` or `bytes.Equal` do, it XORs every byte pair and ORs the results together, then checks the accumulator at the end. So it always touches every byte, and equal-length inputs take the same time whether they differ at byte 0 or byte 30.

The attack it defends against: with a short-circuiting compare, an attacker can measure response times to learn how many leading bytes of their guess are correct — guess `a...`, `b...`, `c...`, and the one that takes microscopically longer shares a first byte with the real key. Repeat per position and the key falls in linear rather than exponential time. Constant-time comparison removes that signal entirely.

One nuance: `ConstantTimeCompare` returns 0 immediately if the slice *lengths* differ, so it leaks length — that's generally considered acceptable, since key length isn't secret in the way its contents are. For your setup (a shared secret checked over a local network) this was always a theoretical concern more than a practical one, but it's the standard idiom and costs nothing.

hookInfos.command: sh -c 'if ! command -v entire >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi; exec entire hooks claude-code stop'

content.text:
Committed and pushed to main as `0df2fd9` ("Harden API key check").

One thing to be aware of: pushing to main triggers the deploy workflow, and the container will now exit at startup if `API_KEY` isn't set in its environment. Per the compose setup it should already be provided, but if the deploy comes up unhealthy, a missing `API_KEY` on the server is the first thing to check — `docker logs tidbit-backend` would show the "API_KEY environment variable must be set" fatal message.
